questions.question: Why does a complete MT examination require two magnetizations in roughly perpendicular directions?
options: To double the amperage delivered to the part
Because leakage flux is strongest when the field crosses a flaw at 45-90 degrees, so any single field direction leaves near-parallel flaws undetected
Because the first shot demagnetizes the part
To satisfy lighting requirements in fluorescent examinations
questions.explanation: A discontinuity leaks flux only when the field crosses it - reliably from about 45 to 90 degrees. Flaws lying parallel to the field barely leak, so full coverage requires a second, roughly perpendicular magnetization.

questions.question: A forged bar is suspected of seams running along its length. Which technique puts the field in the right direction to find them?
options: A coil shot, driving flux along the bar axis
A head shot passing current through the bar, wrapping a circular field around it
A cable wrap of five turns around the bar
An AC yoke with poles placed at the two ends of the bar
questions.explanation: Longitudinal seams run with the bar axis, so they need a circular field crossing them at right angles - current through the part (head shot). Coils, wraps, and end-to-end yokes all drive flux along the axis, parallel to the seams.

questions.question: Your procedure calls for the common prod technique values on a structural weld. With prods set 7 inches apart, what is a reasonable current?
options: About 70 amperes
About 250 amperes
About 700 amperes, from roughly 90-125 amperes per inch of prod spacing
About 7,000 amperes
questions.explanation: Typical procedure values run on the order of 90-125 A per inch of prod spacing; at 7 inches that brackets roughly 630-875 A, so about 700 A is the right order. The written procedure always governs the exact value.

questions.question: Which part is a legitimate candidate for the residual method?
options: A low-carbon structural steel plate, because soft steel holds flux well
A hardened, high-carbon bearing race with high retentivity, verified per the procedure
Any part, provided the bath concentration is doubled
An austenitic stainless steel fitting
questions.explanation: The residual method depends on the field that remains after current stops, so it is restricted to high-retentivity (hard, high-carbon) materials. Soft steels lose their field immediately, and austenitic stainless cannot be magnetized at all.

questions.question: Which statement about magnetizing current types is correct?
options: AC penetrates deepest because it alternates
FWDC gives the best particle mobility of the three
AC concentrates flux at the surface through skin effect and excels at surface flaws, while HWDC adds subsurface reach with strong particle mobility
HWDC cannot be used with dry powder
questions.explanation: Skin effect confines AC flux to the surface, where its pulsation gives particles excellent mobility; HWDC's pulsating DC reaches subsurface flaws and is the classic dry-powder pairing; FWDC penetrates deepest but with the least particle dance.

questions.question: What does a pie (octagon) gauge placed on the part during magnetization actually verify?
options: The exact amperage flowing through the part
The presence and approximate direction of an adequate field at the surface, shown by particle lines across the gauge's segment boundaries
The bath concentration in milliliters per 100 mL
The residual field left after demagnetization
questions.explanation: The pie gauge's brazed segment boundaries act as artificial flaws: particle lines forming across them confirm a field is present and reveal its direction. It is a quick qualitative check - amperage, concentration, and demag are verified by other means.

questions.question: A 30-minute settling test on a fluorescent wet bath should typically read:
options: 0.1 to 0.4 mL of particles per 100 mL of bath
1.2 to 2.4 mL of particles per 100 mL of bath
5 to 10 mL of particles per 100 mL of bath
Zero - fluorescent particles should stay in suspension indefinitely
questions.explanation: Typical procedure values are 0.1-0.4 mL/100 mL for fluorescent baths and 1.2-2.4 mL/100 mL for visible baths. Fluorescent concentrations run leaner because each glowing particle is far easier to see.

questions.question: A crisp particle line appears on a shaft, but it wipes away easily, reappears nowhere near any stress location, and matches the spot where the shaft lay against another magnetized part in the rack. This is best classified as:
options: A relevant indication from a fatigue crack
A false indication from surface roughness
Magnetic writing - a nonrelevant indication from a genuine but flaw-free leakage field
Furring from over-magnetization
questions.explanation: Contact with another magnetized part leaves local poles - magnetic writing. The leakage field is real, so it is nonrelevant rather than false, but it reflects no discontinuity; demagnetizing and retesting makes it vanish.

questions.question: Why must the magnetizing current be switched off before breaking contact at heads, clamps, or prods?
options: To save electricity between shots
Because interrupting thousands of amperes at the contact draws an arc that can flash-burn the operator and leave arc strikes that condemn the part
Because the particles only work when current is off
To allow the gaussmeter to take a stable reading
questions.explanation: Opening a live high-current contact draws an arc - an electrical burn hazard to the operator and a metallurgical injury (arc strike) to the part. Current off before break is an absolute booth rule.

questions.question: Which document specifically governs the qualification and certification of NDT personnel performing MT on aerospace hardware?
options: ASME Section V, Article 7
NAS 410
ASTM E709
The equipment manufacturer's operating manual
questions.explanation: NAS 410 is the aerospace personnel certification standard. ASME V Article 7 and ASTM E709 govern how the examination is performed, not who is qualified to perform it.
